B:
## Recommendation: **Buy** (use a managed auth provider), unless auth itself is your product.

For a B2B product specifically, this isn't a close call most of the time. The reason is that "auth" in B2B doesn't mean a login form — it means the enterprise feature checklist your customers' security teams will demand before they sign. Building that well is a multi-quarter, never-finished commitment that sits far from your actual differentiation.

### Why buy wins for B2B

**The real scope is enterprise identity, not login.** Your buyers will require:
- **SSO** — SAML 2.0 *and* OIDC, tested against Okta, Entra/Azure AD, Google Workspace, Ping, etc. Each IdP has quirks.
- **SCIM** provisioning/deprovisioning (auto-remove the employee who left).
- **Multi-tenancy + org modeling** — users belonging to multiple orgs, per-org config.
- **RBAC** and often custom roles per tenant.
- **Audit logs, MFA, session policies, SOC 2-friendly controls.**

SSO/SCIM alone is the classic "enterprise tax." Providers ship it as a config toggle; building it yourself is weeks per IdP plus ongoing maintenance as they change.

**Security risk is asymmetric.** A bug in your billing code costs you money; a bug in your auth code costs you *every customer's* trust and possibly the company. Providers have dedicated security teams, pen tests, and bug bounties amortized across thousands of customers. You won't match that.

**Compliance leverage.** SOC 2 / ISO 27001 audits get materially easier when identity runs on an already-attested provider. You inherit a chunk of their controls.

**Speed + opportunity cost.** Engineers on auth plumbing are engineers not building the thing customers actually pay for. Buy lets a small team land enterprise deals far sooner.

### When building (or hybrid) is the right call
- **Auth/identity *is* your product** (you're selling an auth platform, IAM, or a dev tool where identity is core).
- **Extreme scale economics** — at very high MAU, per-user pricing can eventually exceed an in-house team's cost. Re-evaluate then, not now.
- **Hard data-residency / air-gapped / regulatory constraints** no vendor satisfies. Even here, prefer self-hosting an open-source server (Keycloak, Ory, Zitadel, SuperTokens, Authentik) over greenfield.
- **Pricing-model risk** — some vendors gate SSO/SCIM behind steep "enterprise" tiers ("SSO tax"). Model your customer mix before committing.

### Practical guidance

| Situation | Pick |
|---|---|
| Default B2B SaaS, want enterprise deals fast | **Managed:** WorkOS (purpose-built for B2B SSO/SCIM), Auth0, Clerk, Stytch |
| Need self-host / data residency / cost control at scale | **Self-host OSS:** Keycloak, Ory, Zitadel, SuperTokens |
| Auth is the product, or truly unique needs | **Build** — but isolate it ruthlessly behind standards (OIDC/OAuth2/SAML) |

**Hedge against lock-in:** whatever you choose, keep your app talking to it through standard protocols (OIDC/OAuth2/SAML) rather than vendor-proprietary SDKs everywhere. That keeps migration possible if pricing or fit changes.

**Bottom line:** Buy a managed B2B-focused provider now. It's faster, safer, and unlocks enterprise revenue sooner — and you can revisit self-hosting later if scale economics or compliance ever flip the math. Treat in-house auth as a deliberate exception you justify, not a default.

One thing that would sharpen this: roughly what scale and customer profile are you targeting (e.g., SMB self-serve vs. large enterprise with security reviews), and do you have hard data-residency requirements? That determines provider choice and whether the cost math ever favors self-hosting.
G:
## Recommendation: Buy

For a B2B product, buy a managed auth provider unless **identity/auth is itself your core product**. Build only the thin layer that's specific to your domain (your permission model's business logic), not the auth primitives.

## Why

**B2B auth requirements are deep and non-negotiable.** Enterprise buyers will demand SSO (SAML + OIDC), SCIM user provisioning/deprovisioning, MFA, audit logs, RBAC, and multi-tenant org management. These aren't nice-to-haves — they block deals. Building all of them properly is many engineer-months, and they're table stakes, not differentiation. You get zero competitive advantage for the effort.

**Auth is never "done."** It's a permanent maintenance and security surface: session handling, token rotation, password reset flows, new SSO quirks per customer's IdP, CVEs. A vendor amortizes that across thousands of customers; you'd carry it alone forever.

**Security blast radius is catastrophic.** An auth bug is a full breach, not a degraded feature. Specialized vendors carry the certifications (SOC 2, ISO 27001) and dedicated security teams — and offload a chunk of liability and compliance audit burden onto a third party you can point to.

**Time-to-market.** Weeks to integrate vs. quarters to build, during which you're not shipping your actual product.

## The nuance

- **If you must control cost/data or have unusual needs**, take the middle path: self-host open source (Keycloak, Ory, Zitadel, SuperTokens) rather than building from scratch. You own deployment but not the protocol implementations.
- **Per-MAU SaaS pricing can bite at scale** — model it early. The "SSO tax" (SSO gated behind enterprise tiers) is real with some vendors; check pricing for the features you'll actually need before committing.
- **Build only when** auth/identity is your product (you're selling an IdP, a fraud/identity layer, etc.) or a hard regulatory/data-residency constraint genuinely rules out every vendor.

## Practical starting point

For B2B specifically, look at **WorkOS** (purpose-built for enterprise SSO/SCIM, pay for what you use), **Auth0/Okta** (mature, broad), or **Clerk/Stytch** (good DX, more B2C-leaning but adding B2B org features). Evaluate against your concrete list: SSO, SCIM, multi-org/tenancy, RBAC granularity, audit logs, and pricing at your projected scale.

**Bottom line:** the build option costs you the most where you can least afford it (security, compliance, opportunity cost) and buys you nothing customers will pay for. Buy the primitives; build only your domain-specific authorization logic on top.
F:
Buy it — for a B2B product specifically, use a managed or self-hostable auth provider rather than building your own. Building only makes sense in a couple of narrow cases I'll flag at the end.

The reasoning comes down to what B2B auth actually requires versus what looks like it requires.

**The hard part of B2B auth isn't login — it's the enterprise surface.** Consumer auth is roughly email/password plus social login, which is genuinely buildable. B2B auth means SAML and OIDC SSO against whatever identity provider each customer happens to use (Okta, Azure AD/Entra, Google Workspace, Ping, ADFS), SCIM for automated user provisioning and deprovisioning, directory sync, multi-tenant org structures, role-based access control, and audit logs. Every one of those is a feature your enterprise buyers will demand in the security review, and every one is a deep, edge-case-ridden spec. SAML in particular is an old, fussy standard where small implementation mistakes become authentication bypass vulnerabilities. This is the category of code you least want to be debugging yourself.

**It's on your critical security path.** An auth bug isn't a normal bug — it's a breach, a disclosure obligation, and a churned enterprise account. Vendors amortize a dedicated security team, pentests, and bug bounties across thousands of customers. You can't match that staffing on auth alone, and auth is almost never your actual product differentiator. Time spent hardening session handling and token rotation is time not spent on the thing customers actually pay you for.

**It accelerates compliance.** SOC 2, MFA enforcement, session policies, and audit trails come substantially pre-built with a serious provider, and many will share their own SOC 2 report to fold into your evidence. Building that from scratch slows down your first enterprise deals, which is usually the worst possible time to be slow.

A practical split on what "buy" means:
- **Managed (Auth0/Okta, WorkOS, Clerk, Stytch, Cognito):** fastest path, least operational burden. WorkOS and Stytch are explicitly B2B/enterprise-SSO-shaped; Clerk leans toward developer experience. Watch per-MAU or per-SSO-connection pricing, which can climb sharply as you land bigger logos.
- **Open-source / self-hostable (Keycloak, Ory, SuperTokens, Authentik, Zitadel):** you host and operate it, but you keep data residency control and avoid per-user pricing. This is the answer when vendor MAU costs would balloon or when regulatory constraints require you to hold the data — you still aren't writing SAML from scratch.

**Build only if** auth itself is your core product (you're selling an identity or access-management tool), or you have a hard regulatory/data-residency requirement that no vendor — including self-hostable ones — can satisfy, or you're at a scale where vendor pricing genuinely dwarfs the fully-loaded cost of an in-house team that can own security forever. For most B2B products none of these hold, and even the "build" cases are usually better served by self-hosting an open-source provider than by greenfield code.

My default for a typical B2B product: start with a managed provider that treats enterprise SSO/SCIM as a first-class feature so you can close enterprise deals immediately, and only revisit toward self-hosted open-source if pricing or data-residency pressure shows up later. If you tell me your stage, team size, and whether you already have enterprise prospects asking for SSO, I can point you at a specific choice.
